citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
There are several bottles of expired medications intermingled with non-expired medications intended for administration to
animals as needed: Heartworm preventative (expired 10/2023), flea and tick preventative (expired 07/2025),
amoxicillin/clavulanate tablets (expired 02/2024 and 02/2023), atipemazole (expired 01/2024 and 03/2025), Adequan
injectable solution (expired 08/2025), trazodone tablets (expired 05/2024) and prednisone tablets (expired 08/2025); there
is a single bottle of cephalexin capsules with no labeling to indicate the expiration date.
The use of expired medications or medications with an unknown expiration date is not considered to be a safe or
appropriate method to prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries because expired drugs may be
ineffective or produce other undesired effects. Animals may experience discomfort or distress due to unrelieved
symptoms or unexpected side effects from the use of expired medication, and anesthetic reversal may not be effective
when needed. The program for providing adequate veterinary care shall include the use of appropriate methods to
prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries. This includes following established procedures to ensure
expired medications are removed and cannot be administered to animals.
TO BE CORRECTED FROM THE DATE OF THIS INSPECTION ON 09/18/2025 AND GOING FORWARD

citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
Inspectors observed an adult female wolf named "Nesta" (ID #303) in thin body condition, with a concave abdominal tuck,
obvious pelvic bones and ribs that are easily seen without fat cover. Facility staff had not noticed that she had lost so
much weight since decreasing her food ration on 07/25/2025. When inspectors noted this and inquired about the animal's
decreased body condition, they increased her food ration.
Failure to perform adequate daily observations can allow problems with animal health and behavior to go unaddressed,
prolonging animal discomfort and suffering, or worsening their condition. Adequate daily observations must be performed,
and there must be a mechanism of direct and frequent communication so that timely and accurate information on
problems of animal health, behavior and well-being is conveyed to the attending veterinarian.
TO BE CORRECTED FROM THE DATE OF THIS INSPECTION ON 09/18/2025 AND GOING FORWARD

***A facility representative reported a recent incident of an adult male wolf, “Quinn” (ID #231), grabbing a child member of
the public’s coat sleeve with his teeth during an encounter inside the animal’s enclosure. It was stated that there was no
harm to the child and thus no incident report written by the facility. The facility states they are no longer doing public
encounters in Quinn’s enclosure until further safety evaluations are conducted.
***It is observed during inspection that an adult female wolf, “Kiara” (ID #274), is allowed to lick the face of members of
the public during encounters while the animal is on a leash held by an attendant.
Failure to minimize risk of harm to animals and members of the public during encounters may result in severe injury or
harm to the animals and/or the public. During public exhibition, any animal must be handled so there is minimal risk of
harm to the animal and to the public, with sufficient distance and/or barriers between the animal and the general viewing
public so as to assure the safety of animals and the public.
CORRECT FROM THE DATE OF THIS INSPECTION ON 09/18/2025 AND GOING FORWARD

***Facility representatives told inspectors that two adult wolf-dog hybrids escaped from their enclosures. An adult female
named “Tippy” (ID #347) escaped on 08/11/2025 by creating holes in her enclosure fencing; an adult male wolf-dog
hybrid named “Summit” (ID#343) escaped on 09/04/2025 by rushing the only entrance when employees were trying to
enter. All of these animals were located within the perimeter fence and returned to enclosures soon afterward or the
following morning. The facility repaired the damaged enclosure walls with wire and additional paneling layers, and the
enclosures are presently in use.
***Two adult wolf-dog hybrids, "Simba" (ID #354) and "Nala" (ID # 355) are inside an enclosure where the wire fencing is
stretched inward in some places from the animals pulling on it.
Failure to construct durable housing facilities and maintain them can result in animal injury and escapes. The licensee
must ensure that facilities are designed and constructed so that they are structurally sound; they must be kept in good
repair, and they must protect the animals from injury, contain the animals securely, and restrict other animals from
entering.
TO BE CORRECTED BY 09/26/2025

There is not adequate provision for drainage and waste disposal to minimize pests, odors and disease hazards. The
outdoor area where raw meat for food is thawed in plastic bins and where bins are subsequently cleaned and stored
directly on the ground has heavy fly activity and patches of earth reddened with discarded blood. The inspectors observed
chunks of meat and fat wastes discarded on the ground next to where food transport buckets are cleaned and stored.
Waste remaining from an elk carcass is stored in plastic bins covered with a towel inside the food storage areas.
Failure to provide adequate drainage or waste disposal may result in injury, disease or discomfort to animals and provide
an environment that fosters pests and vermin that can cause harm and irritation to them. Housing facility operators must
provide for regular and frequent collection, removal, and disposal of animal and food wastes, bedding, debris, garbage,
water, other fluids and wastes, and dead animals, in a manner that minimizes contamination and disease risks. Trash
containers in housing facilities and in food storage and food preparation areas must be leak-proof and must have tightly
fitted lids on them at all times. Dead animals, animal parts, and animal waste must not be kept in food storage or food
preparation areas, food freezers, food refrigerators, or animal areas.
TO BE CORRECTED BY 09/26/2025

In an enclosure housing one adult male wolf-dog hybrid named “Frisco” (ID #331), the plastic shelter provided is too small
for the animal to sit, stand and lie in a normal manner.
Failure to provide dogs with adequate protection from the cold and heat, the direct rays of the sun, and the direct effects
of wind, rain or snow can cause them discomfort, illness or even death. Outdoor shelters without a floor do not adequately
protect dogs from the elements and climatic conditions and may cause them discomfort and distress. The facility must
ensure that all dogs housed outdoors are provided with one or more shelter structures that are accessible to each animal
in each outdoor enclosure, and that are large enough to allow each animal in the shelter structure to sit, stand, and lie in a
normal manner, and to turn about freely; additionally, shelters in outdoor facilities for dogs must contain a roof, four sides,
and a floor.

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
At the time of inspection, there is no running water in the kitchen sink for washing dirty food receptacles, food prep dishes
and utensils. A facility representative explained to the inspectors that these items are being washed outside in a plastic
container (set directly on the dirt ground) with dish soap and heated, 140 degree Fahrenheit water from a hose on the
outside of the kitchen building. These items are allowed to dry outside after washing, and there is no secondary method
for disinfecting these items. The facility moved dishwashing back to the kitchen before the conclusion of the inspection.
Failure to use methods to both clean and sanitize food and water dishes can lead to decontamination of food and water
that may cause animal illness. Food and water receptacles must be not only cleaned, but sanitized, using one of the
methods listed in Part 3, Subpart A, Section 3.11(b)(3); If live steam pressure or water of at least 180 degrees Fahrenheit
and soap is not used, then a combination of detergent/disinfectant products that accomplish the same purpose must be
used, with a thorough cleaning of the surfaces to remove organic material so as to remove all organic material and
mineral buildup and to provide sanitization followed by a clean water rinse.
TO BE CORRECTED BY 09/26/2025

Multiple enclosures are in disrepair including extensively exposed dig barriers, sharp points present, and parts of
enclosure fencing being pulled away from posts due to animal activity:
*** The facility reports an incident where two adult wolves, “Jaeger” (ID #334) and “Sonoma” (ID #340) were able to
escape their temporary, primary enclosure by pulling at the wire wire fence and/or digging recently. Two other adult
wolves, “Aleu” (ID #259) and “Denahi” (ID #260) escaped on 08/04/2025 by creating holes in the wire/chain link fence
walls of their primary enclosure. The facility told the inspector that these animals were contained within the perimeter
fence, captured and safely returned to an enclosure without injury.
***One wolf “Ruhn” (ID# 305) was enclosed in a shift area for feeding, which had an approximately 5-inch long metal wire
sticking straight up from the ground of the dig barrier in the middle of the shift enclosure.
***There is one adult male coyote named “Cody” (ID #317) and 25 adult wolves (“Antoine” (ID #284), “Thistle” (ID #285),
“Sox” (ID #255), “Gambit” (ID #290), “Celeste” (ID #306), “Sable” (ID #337), “Nyx” (ID #314), “Ruhn” (ID #305), “Oakley”
(ID #336), “Cassidy” (ID #328), “Tahoe” (ID #345), “Gray Cloud” (ID #332), “Chappa” (ID #329), “Tioga” (ID #346), “Kirara”
(ID #319), “Korah” (ID #322), “Zion” (ID #280), “Larka” (ID #312), “Gojira” (ID #356), “Gaia” (ID #357), “Asher” (ID #358),
“Sophie” (ID #341), “Aurora” (ID #324), “Poppy” (ID #325) and “Eevee” (ID #323)) inside enclosures with badly exposed
dig barrier that has spaces large enough for toes or feet to pass through.
Failure to construct durable facilities and maintain them can result in animal injury and escapes. The licensee must
ensure that facilities are constructed of such material and of such strength as appropriate for the animals involved;
facilities must be structurally sound and maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury and to contain the
animals.
TO BE CORRECTED BY 10/01/2025

The walk-in refrigerator and freezer unit and area where raw meat is stored have muddy floors and an accumulation of dirt
and debris in a corner, including rodent feces. Failure to maintain food supply stores in facilities with proper upkeep may
result in contamination and deterioration of diets for regulated animals.
Supplies of food and bedding shall be stored in facilities which adequately protect such supplies against deterioration,
molding or contamination by vermin. Refrigeration shall be provided for supplies of perishable food.
TO BE CORRECTED BY 09/26/2025

There is not adequate provision for waste disposal as to minimize pests, odors and disease hazards. The outdoor area
where raw meat for food is thawed in plastic bins and bins are subsequently cleaned and stored directly on the ground
has heavy fly activity and patches of earth reddened with discarded blood and tissue. The inspectors observed chunks of
meat and fat discarded on the ground next to where food transport buckets are cleaned and stored. Failure to provide
adequate waste disposal may result in injury, disease or discomfort to regulated animals.
Provision shall be made for the removal and disposal of animal and food wastes, bedding, dead animals, trash and
debris. Disposal facilities shall be so provided and operated as to minimize vermin infestation, odors, and disease
hazards. The disposal facilities and any disposal of animal and food wastes, bedding dead animals, trash and debris shall
comply with applicable Federal, State, and local laws and regulations relating to pollution control or the protection of the
environment.
TO BE CORRECTED BY 09/26/2025

No artificial shelter was provided for two outdoor housed wolves named “Nyx” (ID #314) and “Gray Cloud” (ID #332), and
two outdoor housed wolves named “Mystique” (ID #230) and “Kabbalah” (ID #229) did not have enough shelter space for
both animals. In an enclosure housing two adult wolves named “Loki” (ID #316) and “Haiku” (ID #313), the plastic shelters
provided are too small for these animals to sit, stand and lie in a normal manner. The natural foliage inside these
enclosures was insufficient for shelter at time of inspection.
Failure to provide adequate shelter for outdoor housed animals may result in their discomfort. Natural or artificial shelter
appropriate to the local climatic conditions for the species concerned shall be provided for all animals kept outdoors to
afford them protection and to prevent discomfort to such animals.
TO BE CORRECTED BY 10/01/2025

Two adult wolves named "Aleu" (ID #259) and "Denahi" (ID #260) are housed inside an enclosure with standing water
covered in a thick, green film.
Failure to provide adequate drainage in animals’ enclosures may result in their discomfort or medical conditions such as
infections. A suitable method shall be provided to rapidly eliminate excess water. The method of drainage shall comply
with applicable Federal, State, and local laws and regulations relating to pollution control or the protection of the
environment.
TO BE CORRECTED BY 09/26/2025
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
Additional Inspectors:
Madeline Anna, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

2.131(c)(1) 
   
Handling of animals.

***A facility representative reported a recent incident of an adult male wolf, “Quinn” (ID #231), grabbing a child member of

CORRECT FROM THE DATE OF THIS INSPECTION ON 09/18/2025 AND GOING FORWARD 
3.1(a) 
   
Housing facilities, general. 
***Facility representatives told inspectors that two adult wolf-dog hybrids escaped from their enclosures. An adult female 
named “Tippy” (ID #347) escaped on 08/11/2025 by creating holes in her enclosure fencing; an adult male wolf-dog

3.1(f) 
   
Housing facilities, general. 
There is not adequate provision for drainage and waste disposal to minimize pests, odors and disease hazards. The 
outdoor area where raw meat for food is thawed in plastic bins and where bins are subsequently cleaned and stored 
directly on the ground has heavy fly activity and patches of earth reddened with discarded blood. The inspectors observed

Failure to provide adequate drainage or waste disposal may result in injury, disease or discomfort to animals and provide 
an environment that fosters pests and vermin that can cause harm and irritation to them. Housing facility operators must 
provide for regular and frequent collection, removal, and disposal of animal and food wastes, bedding, debris, garbage,

preparation areas, food freezers, food refrigerators, or animal areas.  
TO BE CORRECTED BY 09/26/2025 
3.4(b)  Repeat
   
Outdoor housing facilities. 
In an enclosure housing one adult male wolf-dog hybrid named “Frisco” (ID #331), the plastic shelter provided is too small 
for the animal to sit, stand and lie in a normal manner.  
Failure to provide dogs with adequate protection from the cold and heat, the direct rays of the sun, and the direct effects 
of wind, rain or snow can cause them discomfort, illness or even death. Outdoor shelters without a floor do not adequately 
protect dogs from the elements and climatic conditions and may cause them discomfort and distress. The facility must 
ensure that all dogs housed outdoors are provided with one or more shelter structures that are accessible to each animal 
in each outdoor enclosure, and that are large enough to allow each animal in the shelter structure to sit, stand, and lie in a 
normal manner, and to turn about freely; additionally, shelters in outdoor facilities for dogs must contain a roof, four sides, 
and a floor. 
3.11(b)(3) 
   
Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control. 
At the time of inspection, there is no running water in the kitchen sink for washing dirty food receptacles, food prep dishes 
and utensils. A facility representative explained to the inspectors that these items are being washed outside in a plastic

used, with a thorough cleaning of the surfaces to remove organic material so as to remove all organic material and 
mineral buildup and to provide sanitization followed by a clean water rinse. 
TO BE CORRECTED BY 09/26/2025 
3.125(a) 
   
Facilities, general.
